---
title: "Teaching"
description: |
  Course materials for statistics, financial analysis, and more — taught like RPGs.
page-layout: full
---

I run my courses like RPGs. Students earn XP, level up, face boss challenges, and
hopefully leave with skills that outlast the exam. The goal is never to memorize
formulas — it's to build the analytical instincts to *use* them when it counts.

I teach at **ESSCA** (statistics & information systems) and **INSEEC** (financial
analysis, graduate level). Course materials live here and are updated each semester.

## 📚 Current Courses

::::: {.grid}

:::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### [Probabilités & Statistiques](teaching/statistics-probability/index.qmd)

**ESSCA · Undergraduate**

An introduction to probability and statistics as tools for modelling uncertainty
and making sense of data. Covers continuous random variables, classical distributions,
convergence theorems (LLN, CLT), and both descriptive and inferential statistics.

*"Tous les modèles sont faux, mais certains sont utiles."* — George E. P. Box

[Open course →](teaching/statistics-probability/index.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}

:::
:::
::::

:::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
::: {.card .h-100}
::: {.card-body}

### [Financial Analysis](teaching/financial-analysis/index.qmd)

**INSEEC · Graduate (MSc)**

A deep dive into financial and strategic analysis: diagnosing financial statements
(individual and consolidated), profitability, solvency, capital structure, and
return metrics (ROCE, ROE). Covers both listed and unlisted companies, with
real-world case studies.

Part of the *Expert en Stratégie Financière* (RNCP40177) programme.

[Open course →](teaching/financial-analysis/index.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-sm}

:::
:::
::::

:::::

## 🎮 The RPG Approach

Lectures are structured around a **quest narrative**: each session is a chapter,
assignments are side quests, and the final exam is the boss fight. Students earn
experience points (XP) for participation, exercises, and peer review — not just
for grades.

Why? Because the best way to learn statistics or financial analysis is to *do* them
under realistic pressure, with feedback loops tight enough to actually improve. The
XP system makes progress visible, keeps motivation high, and rewards effort — not
just outcomes.

If you're a student looking for slides, datasets, or the leaderboard, navigate via
the sidebar. If you're a colleague curious about the setup, feel free to
[reach out](about.qmd).
